title: Monitoring & Observability
description: Monitoring and observability are how you understand what your systems are doing. Monitoring tells you something is wrong; observability lets you ask why. At Enterprise, every service emits metrics, logs, and traces. This guide covers the Linux-native tooling for system monitoring and the concepts that scale from single-server debugging to fleet-wide observability.

sections.title: The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces
sections.description: Metrics are numeric measurements (CPU, memory, requests per second, error rates) — best for alerting and dashboards. Logs are discrete events with timestamps — best for debugging specific incidents. Traces follow a request across services — best for understanding distributed system behavior. At Enterprise, the 'three pillars' are served by specialized systems: Prometheus (metrics), Elasticsearch/Splunk (logs), Jaeger (traces). On a single Linux system, the same concepts apply with local tools.
sections.codeBlock:
# === CPU Metrics ===
cat /proc/stat                         # Overall CPU stats (user, nice, system, idle, iowait, irq, softirq, steal)
mpstat -P ALL 1                        # Per-CPU breakdown
sar -u 1 5                             # Historical CPU stats (from sysstat package)

# === Memory Metrics ===
cat /proc/meminfo                      # Detailed memory state
free -h                                # Quick summary
sar -r 1 5                             # Historical memory stats

# === Disk Metrics ===
iostat -x 1 5                          # Device-level I/O metrics
sar -b 1 5                             # Historical I/O stats
sar -d 1 5                             # Per-device I/O stats

# === Network Metrics ===
sar -n DEV 1 5                         # Per-interface bandwidth
sar -n TCP,ETCP 1 5                    # TCP stats (connections, retransmits, segments)
ip -s link                             # Interface errors, drops, overruns

# === System Load ===
cat /proc/loadavg                      # Load average (1/5/15 min)
# Load is NOT CPU % — it's the number of processes in running + uninterruptible state
# On an 8-core system: load 8 = fully utilized (each core has 1 task)

# === Top/htop ===
top -b -n 1 | head -20                 # Batch mode (non-interactive)
htop                                   # Interactive with better UX

# === Collect all metrics with a script ===
cat > quick_health.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== UPTIME ==="
uptime
echo "=== CPU ==="
grep 'cpu ' /proc/stat | awk '{usage=($2+$4)*100/($2+$4+$5)} END {print usage "%"}'
echo "=== MEMORY ==="
free -h | grep Mem
echo "=== DISK ==="
df -h / /var/lib /data 2>/dev/null
echo "=== TOP PROCESSES ==="
ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -6
echo "=== NETWORK ==="
ip -s link | grep -E "(eth|ens|enp)" -A 2
EOF

sections.title: Prometheus & Node Exporter
sections.description: Prometheus is the industry standard for metric collection and alerting. It scrapes HTTP endpoints and stores time-series data. Node Exporter exposes Linux system metrics in Prometheus format. At Enterprise, Prometheus is the default metrics system — every service exposes a /metrics endpoint, and node_exporter runs on every server for hardware/OS metrics.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Node Exporter ===
# Install:
sudo apt install prometheus-node-exporter
# Or download from https://prometheus.io/download/

# Node exporter exposes metrics at :9100/metrics
# Key metric groups:
# node_cpu_seconds_total     — CPU time by mode
# node_memory_MemTotal_bytes — Memory stats
# node_disk_io_time_seconds_total — Disk I/O
# node_network_receive_bytes_total — Network
# node_filesystem_avail_bytes — Disk space
# node_load1, node_load5, node_load15 — Load averages

# === Check node exporter metrics ===
curl -s localhost:9100/metrics | head -30

# === PromQL (Prometheus Query Language) examples ===
# CPU utilization (1m average):
# 100 - (avg by(instance) (rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}[1m])) * 100)

# Memory utilization:
# (1 - node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes) * 100

# Disk space > 80%:
# node_filesystem_avail_bytes{mountpoint="/"} / node_filesystem_size_bytes{mountpoint="/"} < 0.2

# === Prometheus configuration (prometheus.yml) ===
# scrape_configs:
#   - job_name: 'node'
#     static_configs:
#       - targets: ['localhost:9100', 'server1:9100', 'server2:9100']

# === Installation ===
sudo apt install prometheus
# Web UI at localhost:9090

# === Alertmanager rules ===
# groups:
#   - name: node_alerts
#     rules:
#       - alert: DiskSpaceLow
#         expr: node_filesystem_avail_bytes{mountpoint="/"} / node_filesystem_size_bytes{mountpoint="/"} < 0.1
#         for: 5m
#         labels: { severity: critical }
#         annotations: { summary: "Disk space < 10% on {{ $labels.instance }}" }

sections.title: Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate
sections.description: Linux has two logging systems: the legacy syslog (text files in /var/log/) and the modern journald (binary, structured). At Enterprise, logs are shipped to centralized systems (Splunk, Elasticsearch, Datadog) — but the local management of logs (rotation, size limits, compression) is still critical to prevent filling the disk.
sections.codeBlock:
# === journald ===
# Centralized logging with structured metadata
# Config: /etc/systemd/journald.conf

# Key settings:
# SystemMaxUse=1G        — Limit total journal disk usage
# MaxRetentionSec=2week  — Auto-delete old logs
# Compress=yes           — Compress old journal files
# ForwardToSyslog=no     — Don't duplicate to syslog

# === logrotate ===
# Rotate, compress, and delete old log files
# Config: /etc/logrotate.conf + /etc/logrotate.d/

# Example: /etc/logrotate.d/nginx
/var/log/nginx/*.log {
    daily
    missingok
    rotate 14           # Keep 14 days
    compress
    delaycompress       # Don't compress newest file
    notifempty
    create 0640 www-data adm
    sharedscripts
    postrotate
        systemctl reload nginx  # Signal nginx to reopen logs
    endscript
}

# Test logrotate:
sudo logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/nginx  # Dry-run (debug)
sudo logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/nginx  # Force run

# === /var/log/ key files ===
# /var/log/syslog          — General system log (Debian/Ubuntu)
# /var/log/messages        — General system log (RHEL/CentOS)
# /var/log/auth.log        — Authentication logs (SSH, sudo)
# /var/log/kern.log        — Kernel messages
# /var/log/dmesg           — Kernel ring buffer
# /var/log/nginx/          — Web server logs
# /var/log/mysql/          — Database logs

# === Centralized logging ===
# rsyslog — forward logs to central server
# /etc/rsyslog.conf:
# *.* @logserver.example.com:514     # Send all logs via UDP
# *.* @@logserver.example.com:514    # Send all logs via TCP (reliable)
# Use TLS for production:
# *.* @@logserver.example.com:6514

# === Log format for machine parsing ===
# JSON logs are preferred at Enterprise (easier to parse)
# Example: logger '{"event":"deploy","service":"myapp","status":"ok"}'

# === Structured logging in applications ===
# KEY=value format for grep-ability:
# 2024-01-15T10:30:00 [INFO] user_id=abc123 action=login duration_ms=42

# Journald structured fields:
journalctl -o verbose | grep -E "(MESSAGE=|SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER=|_PID=)"

sections.title: Distributed Tracing & Observability Concepts
sections.description: On a single server, you track a request through the OS. In a distributed system, you track it across services. Distributed tracing propagates a trace ID across service boundaries, allowing you to reconstruct the full path of a request. At Enterprise, tracing is a first-class concern — every framework and infrastructure component propagates trace context.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Single-server tracing (low-level) ===
strace -f -e trace=network -p <PID>     # Trace network syscalls
perf trace -p <PID>                     # Trace syscalls with perf

# === OpenTelemetry (industry standard) ===
# OpenTelemetry provides:
# - APIs and SDKs for generating traces, metrics, logs
# - Exporters to send data to backends (Jaeger, Prometheus, Datadog)
# - Context propagation (W3C TraceContext, Baggage)

# === Trace context propagation ===
# Trace ID is passed via HTTP headers:
# traceparent: 00-0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c-b7ad6b7169203331-01
#   version-trace_id-span_id-trace_flags

# === Key metrics from tracing ===
# Latency: time spent in each service
# Error rate: failed requests / total requests
# Saturation: utilization of each service
# Apdex: user satisfaction score based on latency thresholds

# === RED Method (for service monitoring) ===
# Rate: requests per second
# Errors: failed requests per second
# Duration: latency (average, p50, p95, p99)

# === USE Method (for resource monitoring) ===
# Utilization: % busy
# Saturation: queue length
# Errors: error count

# === Four Golden Signals (Google SRE) ===
# 1. Latency: time to serve a request
# 2. Traffic: demand on the system
# 3. Errors: rate of failed requests
# 4. Saturation: how 'full' the system is

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'You're on call and get paged for high CPU on a production server. Walk through your response.'

A: 'Acknowledge the page. SSH in (or use session manager). Run uptime to see load averages — is it a spike or sustained? mpstat -P ALL 1 — is one core pegged or all cores? ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head — which process is using the CPU? perf top -p <PID> — which function is hot? If it's a known pattern (e.g., GC in Java, V8 garbage collection in Node), check the application logs. If it's a new pattern, capture a perf record and create a flamegraph. Check if a deploy just happened — rollback if it's a regression. If the process is legitimately busy (traffic spike), add capacity (horizontal scaling) or check if upstream throttling was removed. File a postmortem ticket.'

Q: 'How do you set up monitoring for a new Linux service?'

A: 'First, identify the four golden signals. Install node_exporter to collect OS-level metrics. Add a /metrics endpoint to the application exposing RED metrics (rate, errors, duration). Set up Prometheus to scrape both. Create a Grafana dashboard with: (1) request rate and latency, (2) error rate by status code, (3) CPU/memory/disk of the service process, (4) database connection pool usage if applicable. Set alerts for: p99 latency > 500ms for 5 minutes, error rate > 1% for 5 minutes, disk > 80% full. Test the alerts by simulating the failure conditions. At Enterprise, every service has a 'service level objective' (SLO) — a target for availability or latency — and monitoring measures against that SLO.'

Q: 'What's the difference between monitoring and observability?'

A: 'Monitoring is tracking known failure modes — CPU > 90%, disk full, process not running. It answers 'what is broken?' Observability is the ability to ask arbitrary questions about your system without knowing the answers in advance — 'why is this specific request slow?', 'what is this process waiting for?'. Monitoring requires predefined dashboards and alerts. Observability requires structured data (traces, structured logs, high-cardinality metrics) and tools to explore it. At Enterprise, we invest heavily in observability because the most impactful outages are rarely the things you thought to monitor in advance.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Prometheus Documentation — Official Prometheus docs and best practices
  • OpenTelemetry Documentation — Industry standard for observability
  • Google SRE Book: Monitoring — The four golden signals and monitoring philosophy

learningObjectives: Understand the Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces

analogy.title: Monitoring as Vital Signs
analogy.description: Metrics are heart rate and blood pressure. Logs are the patient history. Traces are the journey of a request through the body. Alerts are the alarm when vitals go critical. The goal is not to collect data - it is to know when to intervene.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Prometheus & Node Exporter
practiceLabs.task: Run `mpstat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man mpstat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate
practiceLabs.task: Run `sar` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sar` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `cat` | The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces |
| `mpstat` | The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces |
| `sar` | The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces |
| `sudo` | Prometheus & Node Exporter |
| `curl` | Prometheus & Node Exporter |
| `daily` | Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate |
| `missingok` | Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate |
| `rotate` | Logging: journald, syslog & Logrotate |
| `strace` | Distributed Tracing & Observability Concepts |
| `perf` | Distributed Tracing & Observability Concepts |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man cat`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: monitoring operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `cat` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the monitoring directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a monitoring directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a monitoring scenario, your monitoring alerts show cat failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  The most important skill in monitoring is knowing what to monitor
FAANG, we don't monitor everything — we monitor the things that tell us whether the system is healthy
productionTips: Production Tip:  Never alert on average latency
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, monitoring & observability is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
